text: hi everyone if you didn't know last
August was a 2024 gmtk game jam it was
one of the biggest game jams of all time
over 32,000 developers competed
submitting over 7,600 games one of those
developers was me and this year I
somehow won the entire Jam this is a
story of how that
happened oh wait if you don't know what
a game Jam is it's an event where lots
of game developers get together to make
games centered around a theme in a
really short time span usually
afterwards everyone plays and rates each
other's games for the gmtk jam the top
100 rated games are then played by Brown
from gmtk from which he picks 20 games
as the official winners of the jam it's
a great time every year and a great way
to get better at Game Dev I have a bit
of a history with the gmtk game Jam
though the first game Jam I ever joined
was gmtk 2020 which was only a week
after I'd really started learning Unity
the theme that year was out of control
so I made a simple puzzle platformer
where you could run out of your controls
it actually did really well during the
jam placing 150 first I had learned a
lot I had a great time making the game
and I appreciated all the feedback I had
gotten so I kept doing game jams after
that but every year after that first Jam
whenever I joined gmtk I kept doing
worse and worse than the year before in
2021 the theme was joined together I
made another puzzle platformer this one
we get chained into a giant ball but I
ran out of time to play test it and make
more mechanics so it ended up pretty
flawed it placed 546 which is still
pretty good but a little disappointing
after the year before in the 2022 Jam
the theme was roll of the dice my idea
for this one was way too big for the
time I had I ended up having to rework
everything at the last minute to get
something playable in time and I
submitted a really boring game about
pushing dice around that ranked
2,718 which honestly was way better than
it deserved three years in I felt like I
learned and improved a lot but it hurt a
ton to see my ratings getting worse and
worse every year it almost felt like I
had peaked a week into learning unity
and I would never reach those Heights
again in 2023 I was actually busy the
week of the jam but if you think about
it not submitting anything basically
means I came in last place so I did even
worse that year what the hell now
winning or even raing well really isn't
the point of game jams they're meant for
fun to challenge yourself and to learn
along the way more than that experts in
any field will tell you that success is
not a linear path it's a dance of
failure and growth and change and you
just have to keep on trying keep moving
forward to achieve your goals but for me
I was on a line path it was a linear
path downwards here's the graph numbers
getting worse and scary oh God what's
happening this year heading into the
gmtk game Jam 2024 I wanted things to
change I've been making games as a hobby
for a long time but for the past year
I've devoted myself to one huge project
I'm trying to release on Steam a puzzle
platformer called inverted Journey which
you should wish this on Steam by the way
it's going to be great trust me working
on such a huge project has dramatically
improved My Game Dev skills and I feel a
need to prove it I know I've gotten
better than I once was and I know I
could do better too looking back on that
first game's ranking I knew I had a
Target to beat so this year I joined the
gmtk game Jam
again the jam this year was 4 days long
and I also had work on Monday and
Tuesday so time was going to be pretty
tight on the Friday the jam started
everyone crowded around the Reveal video
eager to learn the theme and start
development except for me I was at work
but when I got off at 5: I checked the
video and the theme this year was built
to scale my first idea was for a
platformer where you play as a character
that can change size in my head I got
stuck on this image of a puffer fish
character that could puff up and grow
and maybe the force of them puffing up
could launch boxes around or get them
stuck in tight spaces maybe the push
could be physically accurate and and
part an equal force against you so if
you do it against Solid Ground it'll
actually push you away instead kind of
like a physics based jump or wall jump
as I thought through some level ideas
the game headed towards puzzle
platformer territory which I was cool
with I've made uh a couple of those in
the past and I've been making one for
the past year which you should wish L on
Ste by the way and I just like making
puzzle Platformers sometimes okay sue me
I don't have a problem you have a
problem shut up there was an issue with
this idea though
I was planning on making this game in
unity and while unity's physics system
does allow you to resize objects it
can't do it in a way that my game idea
would really want it to in unity
changing the scale of a physics object
will just instantly resize it while
ignoring physics and collisions often
leaving the object half inside the
things around it when this happens Unity
tries to push things around so they stop
intersecting but the results look
terrible don't impart a notable forces
like my idea would want and they break
all the time the problem here is that
Unity uses what's called rigid body
physics which means every physics object
object in the engine is a static shape
that can move and rotate but can't
change its size or shape as part of the
physics calculations this is good for
performance and more than good enough
for most games but for my idea it just
wasn't compatible but a few years ago I
had watched this really great YouTube
video from the creator of the game jelly
car about how he got the physics working
in his game jelly car uses what's called
a soft body physics system which means
it has physics objects that can deform
and change shape when they're acted on
by forces what's better the game even
has a power up that lets you grow your
car to make it bigger in a way that's
entirely physics based
of course all those years ago when I
first watched that video I felt really
inspired and wanted to make my own soft
body game so I tried making one in unity
at the time and it failed horribly
didn't work at all and I gave up but
it's been a while since I've tried that
I've improved a lot and I think this
time I have some ideas on how I can make
it work so I jumped right into it mostly
because I still wasn't sure this would
work at all and I needed to figure that
out right away I spent a while diving
into that jelly cart video and I also
found a bunch of unity tutorials on soft
bodies the jelly car video was great but
it explains a physics system built from
the ground up to use soft bodies and you
need definit is not one of those the
soft body tutorials I found did
integrate with unity physics but they
pretty explicitly mentioned that their
Solutions were inconsistent and might
break at any time but by combining ideas
from these videos and experimenting with
my own methods I eventually landed on
this it doesn't look like much but trust
me this is great you can use a and d to
move around and when you press space the
player will blow up and grow big if I
add a physics object I can push it
around with the growing process if I
grow on the ground it'll push me away
instead and if I grow in a tight space
the player will even grow around corners
and to form in a way that looks great
here's how this works under the hood
when the game starts the player script
spawns in 10 points in a circle pattern
like this each of these are set to be
normal rigid circles using un physics
though they're set to ignore collisions
with each other in order to make sure
that these points will stay together and
in more or less the same shape
throughout the game I then add Unity
spring joints between all the points
Springs are essentially just forces that
want to keep two objects a certain
distance apart by adding Springs between
all these points they'll constantly have
forces pushing them back to the shape
they started as giving the illusion of
one larger shape what's even better any
force that pushes on one of the points
in the in the shape will move it and
compress the Springs heading to every
other point in the shape meaning any
forces applied to any of the points will
be distributed across the whole shape
this looks great but now we need it to
grow luckily this part is pretty simple
when the player holds the space bar are
really big forces added to every point
in the shape pointing outwards from the
center this causes all the points to
shoot outwards up until they get far
enough that the spring force is holding
the shape together overcome that static
outward force that huge Force pushing
outwards is what gives the growing
motion so much power since it sends all
the circles rocketing out letting them
push against boxes and the ground with
tons of force and because of Newton's
third law anytime you push against
something an equal force is applied in
the opposite direction letting you
launch yourself like I wanted next we
need the ability to move around with the
A and B keys this works the way most
games do where you just accelerate the
player's horizontal velocity according
to the inputs with the only difference
being that for my game I accelerate all
10 of the points simultaneously because
the player has 10 colliders and not just
one with this we have most of what we
need but there's a few issues the spring
forces are pretty good at maintaining a
circular shape but they can fail pretty
horribly in some edge cases if I fall
really hard against the floor here the
player glows Splat forever and will
never get up until I grow again this is
pretty funny looking but not that great
since it kind of looks like it died the
problem here is that the Springs only
push the points to be closer or further
apart from other points so when they're
all on the line like this it's
impossible for the Springs to push any
points back upwards out of the floor to
solve this problem I added a secondary
custom spring force that keeps track of
where each point spawn relative to the
center of the shape and then adds a
force pushing that point back towards
that position relative to the player's
current Center every physic step now the
player can still Splat but then they'll
get back up in a way that looks pretty
good after that I spent a good amount of
time tuning all the numbers for the
Springs forces and movement acceleration
to make sure that all the movement felt
really responsive and fun probably my
favorite change during this time was
making the gravity stronger but only
when you're already moving downwards
this is a trick that a lot of
Platformers do to make the controls feel
more responsive and it worked really
well here still it's worth noting that
this solution isn't perfect by any means
probably the biggest issue is that the
circles are the only colliders for the
entire thing which means that any super
thin platform that can fit between them
will just straight up pass through you
my solution for this problem was just to
not make any thin Platforms in the
entire game honestly this was incredibly
workable and it was a great way to save
the time it would take to fix this in
the whole Jam I got one comment that ran
into this issue and the image they sent
about it was really funny so it was
worth it the problem that I should
actually probably fix is that I'm
supposed to be making a game about a
player that grows and right now it's
about a bunch of circles that just move
around so it's time to add some art and
really make this come together the first
thing I did was give it a face a couple
weeks before the gym I'd come across
this cool infographic about Japanese
emoticons which is the word for when you
make faces out of text like these I
thought these were really cute and it's
really easy to add text in unity so
Behold our collection of circles is
officially sentient I want to use more
than just one face out of all these
options so on a whim I decided to make
him change his expression when he puffs
up so it kind of looks like he's holding
his breath in to stay big and Wow it
looked awesome and I love him now great
up next I really have to hide these
circles my original idea was to have you
play as a puffer fish but after
implementing the physics it's it's
looking a little too fluid for that so I
decided to switch the main character to
a slime instead partially because that
would be way easier to draw one of the
tutorials I found use Unity Sprite shape
to make slime characters so I just stole
that idea pretty much all it took was
some Vector math to reassign to Sprite
shapes points and normals to match the
points in the shape and damn that looks
great and it required very little work
God I love everything thank you random
YouTube tutorial I mean it pretty
clearly has some bugs but I can figure
that out later at this point it's 4:00
a.m. I need to sleep and I hit the
hey the next morning I sat down at my
desk and yeah the Sprite shape thing was
too good to be true those bugs I
mentioned are pretty bad Sprite shape is
mostly designed for adding art to
statically shaped objects not for this
crazy soft simulation that goes all over
the place constantly the core issue is
it doesn't like rendering when the
points you give it are too close
together when this happens it reacts one
of two ways either it refuses to accept
the new points I gave it and it stays
where it was lagging behind the player
or it bugs out even worse and stops
rendering entirely for a while these are
both pretty bad because both situations
Reveal Your naked Circle body and people
won't know what they're looking at when
they that I spent a while trying to get
it to work properly before I ultimately
decided that I needed to try another
solution I needed a way to cover up all
the circle points to form a continuous
shape so I dove into learning how to
make arbitrary polygons using code in
unity and I stumbled into unity's mes
rendering system after doing a bunch of
learning about 3D meshes I eventually
made a new script that just renders a
simple polygon onto the points that the
spray shape system was using this never
breaks and consistently covers up the
circles in a way that makes sense but it
didn't look as nice as Sprite shape
since that had the advantage of pretty
looking bezier curves to give a more
rounded shape
I didn't want to waste any more time
improving the polygon solution since I
really need to get on to making this an
actual video game so instead I made a
compromise and I just use both basically
both the Sprite shape and the polygon
attempt to render every frame if
everything goes well the player will see
the pretty Sprite shape solution but if
that fails the polygon will be there to
back it up and cover the circles this
cuts off the worst case scenario of the
player just not rendering and ensures
that something reasonable will always be
shown there is still one visual bug
where the Sprite shape can render but
lag behind the player which leads to
this mildly gross tearing effect but it
looks reasonable enough then I'm going
to just leave it for now and hopefully I
can fix it later also because of sprite
shape my console now looks like this
anytime I run the game but it's a game
Jam game it'll be fine I'll fix it later
trust me don't worry about it I'll

100% okay at this point I really need to

and you get stuck in place this level

sleep all right it's officially Sunday
and I have work on Monday and Tuesday so
it's my last full day of the jam the
next level ideas I had all required
moving platforms so I started by making
those luckily I've made some of these in
my past games the hard part is always
getting the player to stay on the
platform the way I handle this normally
is essentially to add the velocity of
the platform you're standing on to the
velocity of the player this idea sounds
easy Until you realize that typically
the player's movement script exists
which is also trying to set the player's
velocity so it always takes a bit of
retooling to get these systems working
together and in this game it's made more
complicated by the fact that the player
has 10 unique hit boxes of which only
some will ever actually be on the
platform I wasted a lot of time trying a
fancy solution for this but for
ultimately settling on a simpler
algorithm that checks the ground
underneath every grounded player hitbox
and then averages the speed of that
ground and adds that to the player's
movement this ended up working great and
I started working on some levels to make
use of it I made a level to introduce
these platforms and then a few more to
play with the concept I may have gone a
little overboard with the get yourself
stuck somewhere by growing mechanic at
this point since that's a solution for
like five levels in a row but screw it I
like the mechanic and I can do what I
want after all that I had another big

to the older ones it was a good thing I
did this because it turned out I had
made the first moving platform level
obscenely hard so I nerfed it pretty

changes across the game to smooth out

I couldn't stay up super late that day

Fay into them and then quickly realize
oh yeah people spend their entire lives
mastering this skill and I have
absolutely no idea what I'm doing for me
nothing is worse in that way than sound
design I have no understanding of what

and see what sticks at one point I got
